speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me about your teacher, who was a good Nazi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's not so much as he was a good Nazi. I-- he was a-- one of the people that I had met, who had-- who had been a Nazi, who was a member of the Nazi party, who did not participate in any atrocities, and would not have-- who would not have been able to accept the Nazi philosophy. And he was a family man who had an engineering job. He was a young engineer. And the only way he could continue working as an engineer was to join the Nazi party.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell him about the atrocities that you had witnessed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he believe you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he apologize?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh-- he was the kind of person that you knew was deeply hurt--

speaker: interviewer
text: You sensed it?

speaker: interviewee
text: --by what had happened, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did he say it in words?

speaker: interviewee
text: When his wife, overhearing our conversation-- we were talking-- many times ended up talking about other things besides engineering, as it was natural. And he would just shake his head. And then she, one time, wanted to contribute by saying something about, oh, it was so-- so bad. We didn't know. And he just made her be quiet and not to tell somebody like me that they didn't know-- just inappropriate. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: He knew it was the truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back a few steps because there are many of us who, knowing now that you were a Schindler Jew, have seen the movie, as well. So I want to ask you about one or two points of the movie. The issue of experts in-- in-- in weap-- was it weapons or machinery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of machining.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you address that issue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is-- this is simply Schindler's sales pitch.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he said that those-- those-- those women-- that he had to have those particular women who were on the list in-- in Auschwitz, that they must be the ones to sent to Brinnlitz, to his company, because they were experts, and they had specially small hands that would fit inside those shells. And how else could he do this, his job to-- to-- you know to aid the war effort, if he didn't have these experts?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of shells were they?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, in-- in the movie, they are talking about artillery shells. But that's not what we were making. We made--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We made much smaller shells that were used as primers for bombs and torpedoes and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother one of those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother didn't work in the-- in the laundry facilities. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her expertise?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a cleaning lady. And that was here-- she also was an expert. So this simply was Schindler's imagination and his effort at-- to get those people that he wanted to get over to-- to his company.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about you? What was your expertise, according to Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a-- I was a machinist. And I truly did the work as a machinist. And Schindler, for some-- some reason, did-- he probably was amused by having this little boy standing on a box, because I was too short to reach the controls to operate this machine. And that may be why he used to stop and talk to me once in a while, just-- just for-- to be human.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the movie we see also the poignant moment when everyone escorts Schindler outside to his car and he's given a ring. And he spoke-- he speaks to the Jews who he saved. Is that accurate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, he was-- it was accurate that he was given the-- the ring.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave it to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was inscribed. There were-- some of the-- some of our people who worked at Schindler's company were expert jewelers, and so that they did make a ring for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the inscription?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was that 'one who saves one life saves the world.'

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that-- what language was that written in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the words?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I can't repeat them, but I knew the words and I knew the inscription.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that presentation of the ring being given to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't see the presentation in person. Because I was out of this-- this loop. He had a group of adults who were-- who communicated with him. And I was not in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually see him leaving the camp in the car?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- the last time I saw Schindler is when he talked to us in the factory floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he tell you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he told us that-- you know, he told us about the war being over, and that we were free, and so on, and that the guards were leaving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he with his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- no, I didn't see his wife there. And you know, I can recall back in-- in my mind what the situation was. And we were standing on the factory floor. And I was smaller than anybody in there. And we were sort of kind of crowded around. So I had to just take a peek at him, every now and then, in between the bodies. And you know, my most exciting moment-- you know, I didn't believe that all this was over until-- until I was leaving. And when that Russian officer came into the camp and told us, you know, that-- he was on his-- rode in on his horse, and so on. So the scene in that movie was close, very close. Most scenes in that movie were actually a recollection of people who experienced those scene-- those events. And they were recreated. So many of them-- a few things were-- obviously had to be edited in and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, conversations between people could not have been authentic. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Without getting you in too much trouble, was there anything blatantly incorrect?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the movie?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall being annoyed by anything in particular that was incorrect. In my opinion, those who were depicted as camp or ghetto police, Jewish ghetto police, were glossed over too lightly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What should have been said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a little bit of that should have been put in and that these were not the-- your casual friends or your next door neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they were-- in some cases, they were vicious people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, mm hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they called? How were they referred to?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN], [GERMAN]. The inscription was OD. And in German it's [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they forced to be vicious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not really-- not really. That's the sad part of it, of course. But the interesting part is that in a-- under conditions such as these were, they were so crazy and so insane, and not--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was crazy and insane?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the atmosphere. The society, the things that we take for granted in society were nonexistent. And so people who you would not expect to be heroic turned out to be heroic. So you can never tell in advance under high stress who was going to be a good guy, quote, and-- or a bad guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So some of these people were needlessly cruel. And some-- and some, of course, were helpful. You know, it-- it was-- you had a little bit of everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience-- did you ever experience any personal-- any such an attack of cruelty, episode?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, we used to be counted, all the time. And they would-- people were-- these people were in charge of counting the groups. And they were unnecessarily cruel with those people who couldn't line up correctly or something and just whip somebody into-- into a line or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it-- how did it feel to see one Jew whip another?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, I didn't think of it as such, as-- it was just one lousy person--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: Doing-- doing cruel things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were those people ever-- did anyone ever get vengeance, seek vengeance on that those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, those who were obviously cruel and needlessly destructive were then tried as criminals. For sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of any such person-- did you know anyone personally who was in that OD?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did, but he was not one that was later tried. He just simply disappeared from view.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he disappear to? Was he killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He just moved to a-- to an unknown location.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know what you mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because at-- when we first came back to Poland, I-- I saw this person. And I remembered him as being one of the police. And he soon moved, or quickly moved, from a place where he was known. He was not accepted into the society of returning survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Should I ask you what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I do-- I wouldn't know his name right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, those things occurred. They haven't-- as it is normal with human beings, that we had all kinds. We had everything in our society, from philosophers to fools.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you now, when did you arrive in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to-- then we came to America in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you settle?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles. Our relatives, my uncle and aunt, lived in LA, so we came straight to Los Angeles from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going to be 19 that year, 1940-- I was going to be 20. I was 19 in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know we're skipping ahead quite a bit, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK. That's fine. So we came to the United States. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewer
text: No English. First thing we did was go to school and try to learn English. And we went to-- I went to a night school at the Manual Arts High School and took English for foreign born, as did my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell-- and did you tell people your story? Did the Jewish community seek you out in any way, to hear your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't tell anybody my story for the next 48 years, except my-- my own family. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because I didn't think-- you know, by that time I had already experienced a let down. When we were liberated, I was so far off the ground and I thought that from now on, everyone would understand what it means to live together in harmony and all that nonsense that we believe as teenagers, you know. And as soon as I got to Poland, I just dropped down. And I realized that-- that that isn't true. So-- and people were not-- I didn't think people would be interested in knowing what had happened. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you join-- in any way participate in the Jewish community in Los Angeles? Did you join a synagogue at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no I didn't. Not much. I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents did. My parents joined a synagogue. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me that you had enjoyed learning Torah as a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't you feel inclined in any way to go back to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But the need for you know, adjusting to a new country was so great that everything else had to be set aside. So my first-- my first order was to learn English and fit into this new life. You remember, I left my old life behind me. And I-- you know, there were some Polish organizations. People got together and spoke Polish. I only went there once. And while I was there, I realized that it was dumb. It was silly for me to go to places where people speak Polish. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I left. I was-- I was already there. I left there for a reason. I didn't leave there so that I could associate with people who speak Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape six.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, that's a 15 minute marker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, fine. We'll continue then.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married in 1965.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met my wife in the high school where I was working. By that time--

speaker: interviewer
text: One second-- you came to America in 1949. Now when did you come to America?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 19.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't get married until--

speaker: interviewer
text: --much later?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Well, I had things to do. For one thing, it was-- it takes time to adjust. And I needed to learn English so that I can do my work, which I was by that time trained in, the machine shop work. So as soon as I learned a little-- enough English to go to a trade school, I went to Los Angeles Trade Technical College and wanted to convert my skills into the American system. And I did that for a year and a half. And when I finished with that-- by the way, while I was taking these-- while I was going to school, I was also working in a factory. And I was drafted, when my-- when my course was finished. That was during the Korean War. And I was deferred from-- from being drafted in the Army, because I was taking this course. And when the course was over, I was drafted. That was in 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to Korea?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- [LAUGHS] I did. I was in the Army for two years during the Korean War, in the United States Army. I was Army engineer because of they had discovered that I had some training in machine shop work and so forth. So I was in the Army ordnance and then Army engineers and did my training in-- in the United States. And then when we were going to be shipped overseas, before we were shipped, they-- they were asked-- we were asked if there was anybody in the company who spoke foreign languages. And I raised my hand. And I took these tests that the Army gives for people who speak foreign languages. And at the time, I was only in this country two years. So I spoke many languages. I spoke German, and Polish, and Russian by that time. So I took tests in all those foreign European languages. And I passed them all with high proficiency. And when I was ready to ship out, they shipped me to Okinawa. But I spent 16 months on Okinawa during the Korean War.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was in the Army engineers. And I was in charge of post engineers machine shop. And I had 21 Okinawans working for me. And it was quite an experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: You speak Japanese now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the time I could-- I could do quite well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came back to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Came back to the United States and went to-- back to school-- always back to school, on the GI bill this time, back to Los Angeles City College. Did a lot-- had a lot of catching up to do. And I was-- remember, my education was lacking. Even though I learned a lot of English in the Army for two years, but it wasn't anything I could use in mixed company. So [LAUGHS] it was not easy. Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you finally settle down? You didn't get married till you were in your mid 30s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had not been married at all before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you interested in women before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, of course. I was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kept you from getting married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a-- I was an eligible bachelor for some time. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It looks like you're proud of the fact.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was OK. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't your parents want you to get married already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure, sure. I got to where people kept asking me, when are you going to get married? And all my friends-- when are you going to get married? And everyone-- my parents' relations and all that. I got to the point where they stopped asking me about that. Pretty soon they were-- they started asking me, why didn't you ever get married? And then I said, oh, is it that late? Anyway, I waited until I met my-- my wife, who is my-- my wife now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your wife's--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the reason I didn't get married before, because I had never met her.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's very nice. This is the end of--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, this is 10 minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: 10 Minutes, then.

speaker: interviewer
text: 15, 10, remember?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'll tell you what we're going to do. We're going to give you a chance to introduce your wife now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's very nice, very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leon, will you please introduce the women at either side of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is my sister, Aviva. She is from Israel. She lives in Israel now, as I told you earlier. And she came to visit. And she's going to stay with us. And maybe we won't even let her go, and we'll just keep her here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the woman on your other side?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the woman that I told you about, that made me be a bachelor until 1965. See, if she had come earlier, I would have been married earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long are you married then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since 1965.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's?

speaker: interviewee
text: 30 Years.

speaker: interviewee
text: 30 Years. This is Elisabeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elisabeth, has he been a good husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he's just an outstanding human being. So that would mean that he would be a good husband, and a good father, and a good person.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand that you have a good-- a large share in encouraging Leon to tell his story. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when he was ready to, yes. I think that-- but we certainly didn't press it until he was ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you comprehend, when you got married, the enormity of the experience your husband had been through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that anybody who had studied that period of time in history had a concept. But in order to see it in real people, and particularly his parents, who never ever recovered from it, it's-- it makes it very real and very hard to deal with. Leon was younger, so he was able to assimilate more easily into a new culture and a new way of living and to be baptized, so to speak-- probably the wrong word.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think that's the wrong word, if you don't mind. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: To be confirmed, bar mitzvahed by the Army, into American society and a new way of life. That transitional period-- whatever you call it, when you make a change from one kind of life to another. He was a younger person, so it was less traumatic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What lessons does your husband share with you? What lessons based on his-- or Leon, you tell me. What strength do you bring into this marriage and into your life with your wife and your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know. Everything-- every time-- when something comes out, and it turns to be-- turns into something that seems to be very, very serious, and it-- if you reflect on my past experience, it turns out that-- not-- not so serious after all. And that helps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another thing he brings is that he has often spoken about Judaism as being a way of life. And you don't so much preach it as you live it. And he's a perfect example of how everything that he does reflects a world perspective, a human perspective. And he never loses sight of that, even if you catch him off guard, as many people you know, may have a defense or some other kind of instinctive reaction. It's always consistent in what he does.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have found yourself a big fan in your wife. You must've done something very right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can see why I waited until 1965, see?

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you all today. It's been a very long day, but it has been a very big honor for us to hear your story. We admire your courage, your perseverance, and we are honored to hear it today and to share this experience with you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Now, this is my brother and I standing in front of our house in Narewka, Poland. The occasion was that we visited Poland in 1989 and traveled through all of Poland and all the places where I lived. And this is our home-- or was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your names?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my brother, David, and I, Leon Leyson. This is I, on the right side, with two of my friends in Krakow, right after our return in 1945. We were standing in front of a building that received most of the refugees who came back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which building was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was originally a Jewish student housing building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a name of the building?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What street was that on?

speaker: interviewee
text: On [? Premiska, ?] in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't you live on that street?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I lived on [? Premislova. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was [? Premiska. ?] Ah ha. This is a health card. I received that in Wetzlar, Germany in a displaced persons camp where we were all examined and given a health card. This was-- this was 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your hair had grown back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the signature on the bottom-- doctor--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Noe was a Jewish doctor in the-- in the DP camp. You can see DP stamped right there. This was my wedding picture. It was taken in Tarzana at my cousin's house, where we were married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother at the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother's name is Anna at this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anna Leyson.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anna Leyson, Elisabeth, Leon, and my father, Morris.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1965. This was taken in Poland in 1989 on our trip. At the left is my wife, Elisabeth, Daniel-- our son-- Stacy-- our daughter. And up above is my brother, David, my sister, Aviva, and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're the one standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the one standing at the very top.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Leon Leyson. Where was this picture taken? Where in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were on our trip to visit the places where I was in Poland during the war. And specifically, we went to see the common grave in Narewka, Poland where all our relatives are now resting. This was taken in October 1994. In the foreground, you see Tyler Wilfong, being held by his grandfather, Leon Leyson. Next to him is Elisabeth Leyson. Above is Nick Wilfong and David Wilfong and then my daughter, Stacy Wilfong.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Pensacola, Florida, where Stacy and her husband with the two children are now living. This was also taken in 1989. We're standing in a courtyard where I lived in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the city of Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland. And we're in front of the small building that contained the attic, in which my mother and I and two of my friends hid during one of the actions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was standing--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the picture, you see my sister, Aviva Nissenbaum, David Leyson, my brother, and Leon Leyson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does your brother live now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother now lives in Israel.
